summary: builtin_kind::definition/definitions wrap an infallible plain-data lookup in Result<_, KindError> — an empty enum, statically unreachable Err — collapse to bare Option/BTreeMap

edit.description: `KindError` (1480, `pub enum KindError {}`) is an empty enum — its own doc comment (1475-1478) concedes 'every embedded kind is plain Rust data ... none of those lookups can fail: an empty enum, so a `Result::Err` here is statically unreachable.' Once `builtin_kind::definition`/`definitions` (its only remaining users now that `qualified` is pruned, shipped in 84197e5) drop their `Result` wrapper, `KindError` has zero constructor and zero consumer anywhere. Remove the enum and its doc comment.

Re-verified post-84197e5 (the `qualified`-prune ship shifted every line past 509). Drop the now-dead `KindError` import from the `use crate::kind::{ .. };` block (16-19). `definition` (508-510, its own `# Errors` section already concedes 'Never fails; the Result is kept for API stability (every call site already threads `?` through it)') and `definitions` (519-524, same concession) both wrap an infallible lookup over `all_kinds()` — a bare `Vec` literal, no I/O — in `Result<_, KindError>`; every call site across the tree immediately `?`s or `.unwrap()`s it away, never inspecting an `Err` arm nothing can construct. Change `definition` to `pub fn definition(name: &str) -> Option<CustomKind> { all_kinds().into_iter().find(|kind| kind.name == name) }` and `definitions` to `pub fn definitions() -> BTreeMap<String, CustomKind> { all_kinds().into_iter().map(|kind| (kind.name.clone(), kind)).collect() }`, dropping each `Ok(...)` wrap and its now-false `# Errors` doc section. Update this file's own inline test call sites to drop the now-redundant outer `.unwrap()`: 588, 650, 684, 689 (`definitions()`), 713, 748, 794, 834, 874 (each currently `.unwrap().expect(..)` or `.unwrap().is_none()` — drop the outer `.unwrap()`), plus 808, 941, 999, 1022, 1059 (each currently a doubled `.unwrap().unwrap()` — one `.unwrap()` survives, for the inner `Option`).

Two call sites: `let Ok(Some(definition)) = crate::builtin_kind::definition(kind) else { .. }` (317) becomes `let Some(definition) = crate::builtin_kind::definition(kind) else { .. }`; `crate::builtin_kind::definition("skill").unwrap().unwrap()` (402) drops its now-redundant outer `.unwrap()`.
Eight inline-test call sites (720, 721, 901, 974, 998, 1034, 1035, 1036) each drop the now-redundant outer `.unwrap()` off `builtin_kind::definition(...)`, keeping the inner one for the `Option`.

summary: json_manifest.rs's DocumentMember::parse and toml_document.rs's parse each hold a non-exhaustive match over UnitShape, unlike frontmatter.rs's own exhaustive match over the same shared enum

per.section: A shared concept is one type

edit.description: `DocumentMember::parse`'s identity match (187-209) names `UnitShape::NamedField` (188-195) and `UnitShape::File | None` (196-202) explicitly, then folds the remaining two variants — `Directory` and `StarredSegment` — into one `Some(_) => { return Err(JsonManifestError::NoDeclaredIdentity {..}) }` wildcard (203-208). `src/frontmatter.rs`'s own match over the identical `UnitShape` enum (`from_source_rooted`, 192-237, in the same `formats` subsystem) names all four variants (`Directory`/`NamedField`/`StarredSegment`/`File|None`) explicitly — the shape this file's match should hold, per engineering.md's 'a `_` arm over a shared concept is a seam defect waiting for its field report' (the CONTRACT-DECLARED-KEYS-EXHAUSTIVE-MATCH precedent, same bar, a different enum). Replace `Some(_)` with `Some(UnitShape::Directory) | Some(UnitShape::StarredSegment) =>` naming both variants explicitly; behavior is unchanged (both still refuse with `NoDeclaredIdentity`), but a future fifth `UnitShape` variant now fails to compile here until named, matching frontmatter.rs's own guarantee.

`parse`'s identity check (58-63) is a `let Some(UnitShape::NamedField { field }) = &kind.unit_shape else { return Err(TomlDocumentError::NoDeclaredIdentity {..}) };` — the `else` branch is reached by `File`, `Directory`, `StarredSegment`, and `None` alike, none named. Convert to a `match &kind.unit_shape { Some(UnitShape::NamedField { field }) => field, Some(UnitShape::File) | Some(UnitShape::Directory) | Some(UnitShape::StarredSegment) | None => return Err(TomlDocumentError::NoDeclaredIdentity { path: source_file.to_path_buf(), kind: kind.name.clone() }) }`, naming every non-`NamedField` variant explicitly instead of falling through an unnamed `else`; behavior is unchanged (every one of those four cases already refused identically), but a future fifth variant now forces a decision here too.

the Directory-shape refusal (168-171, asserting 'identity shape it cannot serve') stays green — same NoDeclaredIdentity behavior, now reached by a named arm instead of a wildcard

acceptance: json_manifest.rs's DocumentMember::parse and toml_document.rs's parse both name every UnitShape variant explicitly in their identity-selection match, no `_`/catch-all arm or unnamed `else` remains, matching frontmatter.rs's own exhaustive style over the same enum. `cargo test` and `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings` green.

summary: src/drift.rs walks the committed lock's declaration rows three separate times (read_prior_provenance, config_stale, emit_owned_targets) — consolidate to one shared raw-row walk

edit.description: Three fns duplicate the same 'walk the committed lock's declaration rows' job, each independently opening `lock.toml`, tolerant-parsing it as `DocumentMut`, ranging over every top-level `(kind, item)` pair, filtering to `item.as_array_of_tables()`, and pulling columns via `row.get(..).and_then(Item::as_str)`: `read_prior_provenance` (fn 1918, loop 1928-1946, requires name+source_path+emit_hash, returns `Vec<ProvenanceRow>`), `config_stale` (fn 2342, loop 2355-2384, same three columns, inlines the staleness diagnostic instead of returning rows), `emit_owned_targets` (fn 2614, loop 2624-2641, only name+source_path, returns `Vec<EmitOwnedEntry>`). Their doc comments already cross-reference each other's tolerant-absence behavior (1916-1917, 2611-2612) — the duplication is self-aware in the code but never consolidated. Add a private `fn walk_lock_rows(workspace_dir: &Path) -> Vec<RawLockRow>` (raw, all-`Option` columns: kind, name, source_path, emit_hash) that opens and parses the lock once and does the `(kind, item)`/`as_array_of_tables` walk once; have each of the three public fns `filter_map` over its rows instead of re-deriving the walk — `config_stale`'s and `emit_owned_targets`' looser column requirements fall out of a narrower filter each. Same-file fn-level unification — the home stays `drift.rs`, this is not a new module. Consumers (`src/install.rs`, `src/main.rs`, `tests/install.rs`) call only the three public fns — no signature change, no ripple. Re-verified at HEAD 2f44341 (unmoved since b8fc7ca, when observed).

summary: emit() parses lock.toml twice per run — read_prior_provenance (reap-diff) and read_declarations (layer-drop check) each independently open+parse it; hoist to one shared parse

per.section: Cost scale is hoisted, and pinned by count

edit.description: `emit` (fn 901) reads and parses the workspace's `lock.toml` twice in one invocation: the reap-diff loop (1197) calls `read_prior_provenance` (fn 1918, opens+parses the lock at 1919-1925 fresh) and the layer-drop check (1244-1247) calls `read_declarations` (fn 3398, opens+parses the lock again at 3399-3407 via `parse_declarations` -> the private `declarations_from_doc` at 3439, which already takes a parsed `&DocumentMut`). Two independent reads+parses of the same file in one `emit()` run — the whole-input work the cost doctrine hoists once per run and shares, never recomputed per call site. Land after DRIFT-LOCK-ROW-WALK-CONSOLIDATION: that entry's `walk_lock_rows` establishes the doc-based-extractor shape this entry's provenance-row side should follow rather than re-deriving. Fix: parse the lock document once inside `emit()`, then derive the reap-diff provenance rows and the layer-drop nested-member counts from that one parsed `DocumentMut` — `declarations_from_doc` already accepts one; give the provenance side an equivalent doc-scoped extractor. edit.description: `source_deps` (2472) does its own `fs::read_to_string` + `.parse::<DocumentMut>()` of `lock.toml`, independent of `read_declarations`'s (3398) own full parse via `declarations_from_doc` (3439, which already takes a parsed `&DocumentMut` — the doc-scoped-extractor shape DRIFT-EMIT-LOCK-PARSE-HOIST establishes for the provenance side, upstream in this chain). `source_deps` is called by `layout_imports` (2497), `includes` (2508), and — via `source_dep_stale` (2522) — by `layout_import_stale` (2563) and `include_stale` (2580): four independent re-reads/re-parses on top of `read_declarations`'s own. Within one `gate()` invocation (src/main.rs) this lands as 5 full parses of the same file in one run: `read_declarations` (main.rs:858), `layout_imports`+`includes` via `import_edges_from_lock` (main.rs:891), then `layout_import_stale`+`include_stale` (main.rs:1208-1209) — each a fresh read+parse; `explain()` (main.rs:513, 525, 587) repeats the same pattern minus the two staleness calls. `Declarations` (struct 2668) does not currently carry the `layout_import`/`include` lock families read back from disk — `includes: Vec<IncludeRow>` (2691) is documented seam-inbound-only, 'never written back into this declaration table, so a lock round-trip reads it empty' — which is why `source_deps` re-parses independently rather than filtering an already-parsed set. Fix: extend `declarations_from_doc` (or a parallel doc-scoped extractor, the same pattern DRIFT-EMIT-LOCK-PARSE-HOIST establishes) to lift the `[declaration.layout_import]`/`[declaration.include]` families from the one already-parsed document, and have `layout_imports`/`includes`/`source_dep_stale` filter that in-memory set instead of each independently opening and parsing the file. Land after EXTRACT-FOUNDATION-BOUNDARY-RESTORE, the last entry in the existing chain to touch this file, so this entry's extractor sits beside the settled post-move shape rather than racing it.

`gate()` (847) and `explain()` (513) each call `drift::read_declarations` once and then separately call `import_edges_from_lock` (2563, calling `drift::layout_imports` then `drift::includes`) — two more independent parses on top of the first — and `gate()` additionally calls `drift::layout_import_stale`/`drift::include_stale` (1208-1209), two more still. Once `source_deps`'s callees read off the shared parsed document instead, thread the already-parsed document/`Declarations` value through these call sites rather than re-deriving it — the same one-parse-per-run shape `read_declarations`'s own call already establishes for the rest of the declaration family.

summary: gate() recomputes the edge-resolution walk 2-4x per invocation — check's own copy plus resolved_edges/resolved_arcs re-derived by acyclic/degree/mention_reachable — consolidate to one shared walk

per.section: Cost scale is hoisted, and pinned by count

edit.description: `check` (pub fn, 111-129) is a from-scratch copy of the admissible-edge x source x target walk, producing dangling-route `Diagnostic`s. `resolved_edges` (pub(crate), 967-990) is the identical iteration shape producing `Vec<ResolvedEdge>`; `resolved_arcs` (private, 1001-1010) folds a fresh `resolved_edges` call (1006) into adjacency. `acyclic` (195-211) calls `resolved_arcs` at 196 (always-on); `degree` (236-...) calls it again at 252 (opt-in); `mention_reachable` (355-...) calls `resolved_edges` directly at 371 (opt-in) — so one `gate()` invocation recomputes the same walk 2 (check + acyclic, always-on) to 4 times (adding degree/mention_reachable when their selections opt in). Change `resolved_arcs` to fold an already-computed `&[ResolvedEdge]` slice into adjacency instead of calling `resolved_edges` itself (drop the call at 1006); change `acyclic`, `degree`, and `mention_reachable`'s signatures to accept the shared `&[ResolvedEdge]` (computed once by `gate()`) rather than `edges`/`by_kind`, folding/filtering over it. `check` needs the *unresolved* half `resolved_edges` discards (a dangling route is exactly what it skips) — the real design decision, left as a judgment call for build (name the choice in the commit body): either (a) widen `resolved_edges` to return both the resolved set and the dangling occurrences in one pass (a `(Vec<ResolvedEdge>, Vec<Diagnostic>)` or small struct), retiring `check`'s separate loop in favor of reading its half off the shared pass, or (b) give `check` its own filter over a richer per-target enumeration `resolved_edges` derives from, keeping it a thin projection rather than a parallel walk. Whichever shape lands, reword `resolved_edges`'s doc comment (964-966) and the module header's READ-EDGE-UNIFY claim (11-13, 'one resolved-edge enumeration... shared') to state the runtime-sharing this consolidation establishes, not just the type-sharing it already had.

`gate()` (847) calls `graph::check` (1131, always-on), `graph::acyclic` (1147, always-on), `graph::degree` (1154, opt-in), and `graph::mention_reachable` (1162, opt-in) — four independent triggers of graph.rs's walk today. Once graph.rs's functions take the shared `&[ResolvedEdge]` slice (or the widened resolved+dangling shape), compute it once here, after `graph::admissibility` (1130) and before the four consumers, and thread it into each call, replacing the `edges`/`by_kind` pair those calls pass today wherever the walk itself moved to the caller.

`why`'s narration (`resolved` at 335) calls `graph::resolved_edges(edges, by_kind)` directly — a separate verb invocation from `gate()`'s own run, so it is not part of the within-gate duplication this entry fixes and needs no consolidation of its own. It is `resolved_edges`'s only consumer outside graph.rs (doc-comment cites at 21, 62, 309, 470) — if the design decision above widens `resolved_edges`'s return shape, this call site's usage narrows to the resolved half; verify at implementation time whether the signature change ripples here, and update the call plus the four doc-comment cites to match whatever shape lands.

summary: InstallEntry.placement is four bare &str consts matched with a wildcard fallback in gate_installed — the MODELINE case is never named, only reached via `_`

per.section: A shared concept is one type

edit.description: `InstallEntry.placement: &'static str` (370) is one of four consts — `SESSION_START` (91), `MODELINE` (93), `GUARD_HOOK` (95), `NOTE` (97) — set at its five construction sites (582, 606, 612, 642, 656). `gate_installed`'s tally (522-533) matches them as `SESSION_START => hook = true, GUARD_HOOK => guard = true, NOTE => notes += 1, _ => modelines += 1` — `MODELINE` is never named in the match, reached only through the `_` fallback, which would also silently absorb any future fifth placement as a modeline, uncaught by the compiler. This is the shared-concept posture's own described failure shape ('a `_` arm over a shared concept is a seam defect waiting for its field report'). Replace the four string consts and the `&'static str` field with a real `#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)] pub enum Placement { SessionStart, Modeline, GuardHook, Note }`, update the five construction sites and `gate_installed`'s match to name every variant exhaustively (no `_` arm), and give `Placement` a `Display`/label method carrying today's exact strings ('session-start hook', 'schema modeline', 'guard hook', 'managed-by note') for the two `.to_string()` uses at 540/543 and the report line built from it.

`outcome_of` (137-147) and `has_entry` (150-152) take `placement: &str` and compare with `==` against `entry.placement`; ~15 call sites pass string literals (`"session-start hook"` 233/430/544, `"guard hook"` 236/428/717/751/897/918, `"managed-by note"` 237, `"schema modeline"` 238/440/790). Once `InstallEntry.placement` becomes `Placement`, both helpers' signatures change to `placement: Placement` and every call site's string literal becomes the matching enum variant (`Placement::SessionStart`, `Placement::GuardHook`, `Placement::Note`, `Placement::Modeline`).

acceptance: InstallEntry.placement is a real Placement enum with an exhaustive match in gate_installed (no `_` arm); the four bare &str consts are gone. cargo test and cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings green.

edit.description: `requireSections = (): Predicate => ({ key: "require_sections" })` (168) takes no argument even though its own doc comment reads 'The named headings are present' — unlike `dependencyExists` (whose missing constructor is explicitly documented: 'the engine holds it back absent a decidable reference syntax'), nothing here names this as a deliberate hold. Change the signature to `requireSections = (sections: readonly string[]): Predicate => ({ key: "require_sections", sections })`. Add a `sections?: readonly string[]` field to the `Predicate` interface (76, beside `section`), documented the same way `keys`/`values` are ('`require_sections`'s required heading list').

`clauseRow` (65-115) maps each `Predicate` field to its `ClauseRow` column by explicit `predicate.key === "..."` branches (`keys`, `values`, `section`, etc. — no generic spread). Add: `sections: predicate.key === "require_sections" && predicate.sections ? [...predicate.sections] : undefined,` alongside the existing `keys`/`values`/`section` branches (110-114), copying into a fresh array per the file's own documented reason ('the generated rows carry mutable columns; the predicate's ... is read-only, so copy each into a fresh array').

`ClauseRow` (struct at 2869-2971) carries no column a `require_sections` row could use — `keys`/`values`/`section` are documented as each other predicate's own. Add a new field `pub sections: Option<Vec<String>>,` after `section` (2970), with a doc comment naming it `require_sections`'s required-heading-list column, matching the `#[serde(default)]` pattern every other optional column carries. `predicate_from_row` (424-...) has arms for every predicate key except `require_sections` and the constructor-less `dependency-exists` (falls through `_ => return None`). Add, near the `must_define` (458) / `section_contains` (461) arms: `"require_sections" => Predicate::RequireSections { sections: row.sections.clone()? },`.

tests.asserts: a new test asserts requireSections(["Usage", "Decision"]) composes { key: "require_sections", sections: ["Usage", "Decision"] } as an ordinary Predicate value, the pattern the file's other node-set constructor tests (count, unique, membership) already follow

summary: `MAX_IMPORT_HOPS = 5` cites a memory doc that now reads four hops: the gate constant and its own source disagree, and the doc sentence cannot settle which counting is temper's

gate.reason: A human probe rules the hop semantics — the doc text cannot. Fetched live at 420da04 (code.claude.com/docs/en/memory, retrieved 2026-07-16): 'Imported files can recursively import other files, with a maximum depth of four hops.' Both readings survive that sentence: the root's own import is hop 1 and the chain caps at four edges (⇒ 4), or 'imported files ... recursively import' names a recursion four deep ATOP that first import (⇒ 5, today's constant). Only measuring the runtime's actual load depth decides it, the headless-testbed act that produced the `(mention-gate-containment)` evidence — never plan's to guess and never build's to fetch, because guessing here edits a live gate's semantics: a cap too low forges a dead-member finding on a member that loads, a cap too high suppresses a real one (intent.md invariant 2).

edit.description: The constant and the cite that justifies it. `MAX_IMPORT_HOPS = 5` (59) sits under a doc (55-58) asserting 'the `at-import` grammar is recursion-capped at five hops (code.claude.com/docs/en/memory, retrieved 2026-07-02)' — a number the live page does not say. temper's own indexing IS decidable on disk and needs no ruling: `live_members` (630) seeds every own-live member and runs `for _ in 0..MAX_IMPORT_HOPS` BFS rounds (657), one import edge per round, so the constant counts EDGES traversed from a live seed. What no reading of the disk settles is which edges the doc's 'four hops' counts — hence the park. Whatever the ruling: the cite is re-retrieved, re-dated, and re-worded to state the hop-counting it encodes, never left asserting a bare number its source does not carry. The two narration cites deferring to it — 528 ('hop-capped at [`MAX_IMPORT_HOPS`]') and 626-629 ('capped at [`MAX_IMPORT_HOPS`] hops (the `at-import` recursion depth the format documents)') — carry the same claim and move with it, as does the propagation comment at 652-655 ('capped at the format's import recursion depth'). 6667265 (mention-reachable, in the b85df4a..HEAD window) shifted every address after ~519 — the const (59) and its doc (55-58) sit above the edit and are unmoved; the later addresses re-read at HEAD, so every address carries.

`the_import_closure_is_hop_capped` (1407) already pins the boundary mechanically rather than in a comment — `r1..r5` rescued from a live seed, `r6` dead as the sole finding — so it encodes today's 5 in the one place a flip cannot silently miss, and it is the test that moves if the ruling is four. Its comment (1408-1412) spells 'capped at the format's five-hop import recursion' (1410) and carries the same claim as the constant's cite; re-word it to the ruled reading rather than leaving the fixture narrating the retired number. 6667265 (mention-reachable, in the b85df4a..HEAD window) added the mention-reachable fixture and shifted this test down; addresses re-read at HEAD, so they carry.

tests.asserts: the import closure rescues a chain exactly as deep as the ruled cap and no deeper — the boundary hop pinned at the ruled number, in the ruled counting

acceptance: `MAX_IMPORT_HOPS`, its doc cite, and the two narration cites that defer to it all agree with the live docs, and each states which hop-counting it encodes (spec-system.md, 'Form rules': an external fact carries its source and retrieval date where it is enforced, verified against current docs, never encoded from memory). The boundary hop stays pinned by a test at the ruled number. `cargo test` green.
notes: Park re-tested at d1af9a5 and holds — nothing ruled the hop semantics; VERIFIER-TYPED's window touched graph.rs but not the const region (still 5 at graph.rs:59, cite still 2026-07-02 at 55-58). The park's subject is untouched, and may still be wrong. A build-side friction capture records the same disagreement; the human's to read, not plan's to drain. rescoped 4a33911.

summary: graph.rs's declared_globs and engine.rs's field_globs duplicate one glob-extraction job with a trim/filter drift — consolidating needs a ruling on whether glob-valid adopts that semantics

gate.reason: declared_globs (src/graph.rs:780-796, doc 774-779) trims each glob and drops blank/whitespace-only entries — its own doc: 'an absent/blank paths field falls back to unconditional loading' (pinned by tests/graph.rs:1466, an_absent_or_blank_paths_field_is_reachable). field_globs (src/engine.rs:1137-1143) does neither, feeding glob-valid's compile_glob check (engine.rs:922) unfiltered. Unifying into one extractor is a product call, not a shape call: does glob-valid start flagging a blank/whitespace-only paths entry as a syntax problem (adopting the trim/filter semantics), or does the reachability judge's fallback-to-unconditional treatment stay its own business, with field_globs's no-op behavior the one both share instead? A human names which semantics the shared extractor holds before this lands — plan does not invent it (collaboration.md, 'Challenge gaps, never paper over them').

edit.description: `declared_globs` (780-796, doc 774-779: 'The registration globs a member declares on `field` ... Declaring none is *not* a dead edge') and engine.rs's `field_globs` (1137-1143, doc 1134-1136) both extract the glob strings a `FeatureValue` field carries — `declared_globs` trims each glob and drops blank/whitespace-only entries; `field_globs` does neither. `declared_globs` feeds this file's reachability judge (`reachable`, blank-field-falls-back-to-unconditional pinned at tests/graph.rs:1466). Once the parked ruling names the shared semantics, land one shared extractor (this file, or a leaf both files call) implementing the ruled behavior, and route the reachability judge through it — collapsing `declared_globs`'s body into the shared fn if the ruling keeps trim/filter semantics, or updating its call sites if the ruling instead standardizes on field_globs's no-op behavior.

`field_globs` (1137-1143) is the glob-string extractor the `glob-valid` predicate arm (915-923, feeding `crate::kind::compile_glob`) calls — pinned by `glob_valid_fires_once_per_unparseable_glob_and_passes_valid_brace_globs` (2168). Once the parked ruling names the shared trim/filter semantics, remove this private duplicate and route the glob-valid arm (922) through the shared extractor graph.rs now hosts (or whatever home the ruling settles), updating the one call site.

tests.asserts: an_absent_or_blank_paths_field_is_reachable (1466) stays green under whichever semantics the ruling settles — a blank paths field's registration edge is treated the reachability judge's ruled way, explicitly, not silently inherited from the merge

glob_valid_fires_once_per_unparseable_glob_and_passes_valid_brace_globs (2168) stays green, or is deliberately extended with a blank/whitespace-only-glob case if the ruling makes glob-valid start flagging it — named in the commit body either way

acceptance: One shared glob-string extractor serves both graph.rs's reachability judge and engine.rs's glob-valid predicate, implementing the human-ruled trim/filter semantics; declared_globs and field_globs collapse to the one implementation. `cargo test` and `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings` green.
